data.title: Asia markets rise as trade talks eyed; investors assess economic data in the region
data.description:
Asia-Pacific stocks mixed on Thursday, following an overnight sell-off on Wall Street sparked by Fed Chair Powell’s warning that trade tensions could threaten the Fed’s inflation and employment goals.

Confusion arose after a White House statement indicated that certain Chinese goods may face tariffs of up to 245%, encompassing both new and historical levies dating back to the first Trump administration. However, markets were calmed somewhat by clarification that the new tariffs stand at 145%.

In corporate news, investors awaited Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co’s (TSMC) first-quarter earnings due later in the day.

Gold hovered at an all-time high above the $3,340 level on Thursday.

Japan (NKY:IND) rose 0.86% to above 34,000 while the broader Topix Index gained 0.3% to 2,505 on Thursday, recouping losses from the previous session, with technology stocks leading the rebound. The Japanese yen weakened past 142 per dollar on Thursday, pulling back from multi-month highs as the US dollar regained strength following hawkish remarks from Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell.

Tokyo officials are in Washington this week pushing for the full removal of President Trump’s tariffs, including a 10% base tariff and an additional 25% levy on Japanese car exports.

Despite the focus on trade, Economy Minister Ryosei Akazawa noted that foreign exchange issues have not been part of the discussions.

Bank of Japan (BoJ) board member Junko Nakagawa stated in a speech that U.S. trade policy represents a substantial risk to Japan's economic prospects. 

On the economic front, Japan’s March trade surplus above forecasts as export growth outpaced imports, weighed down by soft demand from China and the EU. 

China (SHCOMP) rose 0.01% held above 3,270 while the Shenzhen Component rose 0.3% to 9,800 on Thursday, with mainland stocks remaining supported following reports that China is open to engage in trade negotiations with the US with certain preconditions, and the offshore yuan to around 7.31 per dollar on Thursday, reversing gains from the previous session as investor caution resurfaced amid renewed concerns over US-China trade relations. 

China's foreign ministry on Thursday dismissed the U.S.'s "tariff numbers game" after the White House detailed how China's retaliation leads to tariffs up to 245%, including reciprocal, fentanyl, and unfair trade practice duties. This follows Trump's recent global tariffs, largely eased except for China.

Meanwhile, Washington stated Trump is open to a deal, but that Beijing must take the first step, adding China “needs our money.” Other countries are now exploring bilateral trade pacts with the U.S.

Hong Kong (HSI) rose 1.33% to 21,324 in early trade Thursday, buoyed by broad-based sector gains.

India (SENSEX) fell 0.07% to 76,971 in early trade on Thursday, halting gains from the previous three sessions amid sharp losses in tech stocks.

Australia (AS51) rose 0.59% to around 7,770 on Thursday, recovering losses from the previous session. The Australian dollar slipped to around $0.635 on Thursday, breaking a six-day winning streak, as weaker-than-expected employment data fueled expectations of further monetary easing by the Reserve Bank of Australia.

While the unemployment rate stayed low at 4.1%, March employment growth came in below forecasts.

The Bank of Korea maintained its base rate at 2.75% during its April 2025 meeting, following a 25 bps cut in February aimed at stabilizing the currency amid new U.S. tariffs, aligning with market expectations.

In the U.S., on Wednesday, all three major indexes ended lower, led by a steep downturn in tech stocks as trade tensions escalated and investors digested cautious remarks from Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell. 

In a speech on Wednesday, Chair Powell's comments in Chicago added pressure on markets, as he warned that rising tariffs could both fuel inflation and dampen growth, complicating the Fed's dual mandate. His ambiguous stance on interest rates further unsettled investors, causing major indices to hit session lows.

U.S. stock futures stabilized on Thursday after a sharp tech-driven selloff in the previous session: Dow +0.69%; S&P 500 +0.76%; Nasdaq +0.84%.

Currencies: (JPY:USD), (CNY:USD), (AUD:USD), (INR:USD), (HKD:USD), (NZD:USD).

More on Asia:

  • China's March retail sales rises more than expected to 5.9%; industrial output rises 7.7%; unemployment rate falls 5.2%

  • China's Q1 GDP expands 1.20% Q/Q

  • RBA minutes reveal cautious approach to future interest rate moves

  • China's March trade surplus above estimates, exports surge while imports fall

  • Trump intensifies tariff pressure: "No one off the hook," denies tariff rollbacks

data.title: Woodside signs LNG supply agreements With Uniper
data.description:
Woodside (NYSE:WDS) on Wednesday said that it had signed LNG sale and purchase agreements with Uniper (OTCPK:UNPRF) amid Europe's rising energy demand.

Louisiana LNG LLC will supply 1.0 Mtpa of LNG on a free-on-board basis for up to thirteen years from the commercial operations date (COD) of Louisiana LNG.

In addition, Woodside Energy Trading Singapore Pty. Ltd. will supply up to 1.0 Mtpa of LNG on a delivered ex-ship basis from Woodside’s global portfolio into Europe commencing with Louisiana LNG’s COD over a term until 2039.

The sale and purchase agreements are subject to Woodside’s (NYSE:WDS) final investment decision on the three train 16.5 Mtpa foundation development of Louisiana LNG.

Woodside CEO Meg O’Neill said these agreements represent another important milestone to move Louisiana LNG towards a final investment decision.

“As we continue to progress sell-down opportunities for Louisiana LNG, these agreements reinforce the project’s unique advantages and its economic competitiveness,”  Meg O’Neill said.

“In an environment of increasing demand for dependable sources of LNG, particularly in Europe, we remain focused on delivering reliable energy supply that will benefit our partners and stakeholders for years to come,” she said.

Sources: Press Release

data.title: Donald Trump's admin says Harvard may lose foreign student enrollment
Australia's unemployment rate stays low at 4.1%, March employment growth below forecasts
data.description:
Australia’s seasonally adjusted unemployment rate inched up to 4.1% in March 2025 from a downwardly revised 4% in February, but below market estimates of 4.2%.

The number of unemployed people increased by 3 thousand to 613,900, with those seeking full-time jobs climbing 11.2 thousand to 422.6 thousand while those looking for part-time jobs fell by 8.1 thousand to 191.3 thousand. 

The underemployment rate stood at 5.9%. Monthly hours worked in all jobs dropped by 6 million to 1,965 million.

Meanwhile, employment in Australia rose by 32.2 thousand to 14.54 million in March 2025, less than market forecasts of a 40 thousand increase, the rebound followed a revised 57.4 thousand decline in February, the first drop in 11 months. 

Over the year to March, total employment expanded by 308.0 thousand or 2.2%. This annual growth rate is slightly higher than the 20-year pre-pandemic average of 2.0%.

The S&P/ASX 200 Index rose 0.1% to around 7,770 on Thursday, recovering losses from the previous session. The Australian dollar slipped to around $0.635 on Thursday, breaking a six-day winning streak, as weaker-than-expected employment data fueled expectations of further monetary easing by the Reserve Bank of Australia.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:EWA), (NYSEARCA:FXA), (NYSEARCA:FLAU).

More on Australia

  • Australia's trade surplus shrinks to 2020 low; imports surge, exports fall

  • Australia’s monthly CPI eased to three-month low of 2.4% in February

  • Australia's Feb unemployment rate remains unchanged at 4.1%, aligning with expectations

  • Australia’s trade balance exceeds expectations, export growth accelerates and imports decline

  • RBA cuts its cash rate by 25 bps for first time since 2020, in-line with market expectations

data.title: Japan’s March trade surplus above forecasts as export growth outpaced imports
data.description:
Japan's trade surplus saw a considerable increase in March 2025, reaching JPY 544.1 billion; notably higher than the JPY 349.9 billion surplus recorded in the same month of the previous year and also surpassed market forecasts, which had anticipated a surplus of JPY 485.3 billion.

The positive result was driven by the fact that Japan's export growth outpaced its imports during the month.

Japan's exports increased by 3.9% y/y to JPY 9,847.8 billion in March 2025, below market forecasts of 4.5%, markind a sharp slowdown from an 11.4% jump in the previous month, influenced by U.S. steel and aluminum tariffs. 

Japan's imports grew by 2.0% yoy to JPY 9,303.8 billion in March 2025, recovering from a 0.7% decline in the previous month but falling short of the projected 3.1% rise.

Meanwhile, Japan’s Economy Minister Ryosei Akazawa said that foreign exchange was not discussed during ongoing trade talks in Washington.

Bank of Japan (BoJ) board member Junko Nakagawa stated in a speech that U.S. trade policy represents a substantial risk to Japan's economic prospects. She further cautioned that increased U.S. tariffs could have a direct detrimental effect on Japanese corporate activities, impacting areas such as exports, production levels, capital expenditure, sales figures, and overall profitability.

The Nikkei 225 Index rose 0.4% to above 34,000 while the broader Topix Index gained 0.3% to 2,505 on Thursday, recouping losses from the previous session, with technology stocks leading the rebound. The Japanese yen weakened past 142 per dollar on Thursday, pulling back from multi-month highs as the US dollar regained strength following hawkish remarks from Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell.

More on Japan

  • Japan producer prices rise more than expected to 4.2% in March

  • Japan’s unemployment rate unexpectedly drops to 2.4% in February

  • Japan's Feb industrial production exceeds expectations, retail sales fall short of forecasts
  • Japan’s manufacturing downturn extends to ninth month; services activity turns negative
  • Japan’s core inflation rate slows to 3% in February, exceeds forecast; food prices slow slightly

data.title: Thursday's Economic Calendar
data.description:
8:30 AM Housing Starts and Permits 
 
Housing starts measure the initial construction of single-family and multi-family units on a monthly basis. 
 
The call for March is a 1.42 million unit rate, down from 1.501 million in February when starts outperformed expectations. Permits are seen at a 1.45 million unit rate in March versus 1.456 million in February.
 
8:30 AM Jobless Claims 
 
New unemployment claims are compiled weekly to show the number of individuals who filed for unemployment insurance for the first time. 
 
Claims are seen up to 225K after rising by 4K to 223K in the previous week. The 4-week moving average was 223K last week.
 
8:30 AM Philadelphia Fed Manufacturing Index 
 
The general conditions index from this business outlook survey is a diffusion index of manufacturing conditions within the Philadelphia Federal Reserve district.
 
The index, an early read on manufacturing for April, is expected at 6.7 from 12.5 in March and 18.1 in February. 
 
10:30 AM EIA Natural Gas Report 
 
The Energy Information Administration (EIA) provides weekly information on natural gas stocks in underground storage for the U.S. and five regions of the country. The level of inventories helps determine prices for natural gas products.
 
11:45 AM Fed's Barr Speech 
 
Federal Reserve Board Governor Michael Barr will speak on"Cybersecurity in the Banking System" and participate in moderated discussion before the State-of-the-Field Conference on Cyber Risk to Financial Stability.
 
1:00 PM Baker Hughes Rig Count
 
The Baker Hughes North American rig count tracks weekly changes in the number of active operating oil & gas rigs. 
 
4:30 PM Fed Balance Sheet 
 
The Fed balance sheet is a weekly report listing the assets and liabilities of the Federal Reserve System.

data.title: Alcoa sees $105M quarterly tariff impact and anticipates $100M annual net loss from U.S. tariffs
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Alcoa Corporation (AA) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO William Oplinger highlighted strong operational excellence in Q1 2025, citing no fatal or serious injuries, improved production stability across most operations, and the Alumar smelter in Brazil reaching 91% capacity. He noted a $1 billion debt offering in Australia, which extended maturity and reduced interest costs. Additionally, the company formed a joint venture with IGNIS EQT to resume production at the San Ciprian smelter.

  • CFO Molly Beerman reported Q1 2025 revenue of $3.4 billion, a 3% decrease sequentially. Net income attributable to Alcoa was $548 million with an EPS of $2.07 per share. Adjusted EBITDA rose by $178 million to $855 million, driven by higher aluminum prices and reduced intersegment profit eliminations, offset by increased alumina costs and tariffs on Canadian aluminum imports.

  • CFO Beerman elaborated on the $105 million quarterly impact from U.S. Section 232 tariffs, detailing the cost structure and Midwest premium assumptions.

Outlook

  • CFO Beerman updated Q2 2025 guidance, projecting unfavorable performance in the Aluminum segment by approximately $105 million due to increased tariff impacts and operational costs from the San Ciprian smelter restart. Lower alumina prices are expected to reduce overall adjusted EBITDA, but alumina costs within the Aluminum segment should improve by $165 million.

  • The full-year depreciation forecast was revised down to $620 million from $640 million due to favorable currency effects.

  • CEO Oplinger discussed ongoing efforts to address tariffs, highlighting the estimated $400 million to $425 million annual cost increase due to the 25% tariff on Canadian aluminum imports, resulting in a net negative impact of approximately $100 million annually after accounting for higher Midwest premiums.

Financial Results

  • Q1 2025 revenue declined 3% sequentially, attributed to lower shipments and alumina price impacts. The Alumina segment saw a revenue decrease of 8%, while the Aluminum segment remained flat.

  • Adjusted EBITDA increased by $178 million to $855 million, supported by favorable pricing mix and cost improvements, despite rising alumina costs and U.S. tariff expenses.

  • Free cash flow remained positive, with a cash balance of $1.2 billion at quarter-end. Working capital increased, driven by higher inventories and raw material costs.

Q&A

  • Timna Tanners, Wolfe Research, asked about the distinction between the $105 million quarterly tariff impact and the $100 million annualized net impact. CFO Beerman clarified that the $100 million annual impact considers the offsetting higher Midwest premium and Canadian sales values, while the $105 million is based on quarterly projections with specific LME and Midwest premium assumptions.

  • Chris LaFemina, Jefferies, inquired about the financial risks of the San Ciprian smelter restart. CFO Beerman indicated expected EBITDA losses of $70 million to $90 million in 2025, with hedging strategies in place to mitigate risks for 2026 and beyond.

  • Analysts raised concerns over aluminum tariffs and their long-term impact. CEO Oplinger emphasized ongoing engagement with U.S. and Canadian policymakers to advocate for favorable trade conditions.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts displayed a neutral to slightly negative tone, expressing skepticism about the tariff impacts and financial risks associated with the San Ciprian restart. Concerns were voiced regarding clarity on tariff stickiness and its broader implications.

  • Management maintained a confident tone during prepared remarks, emphasizing operational achievements and strategic actions. However, during the Q&A, their tone became more defensive, particularly when discussing tariff impacts and San Ciprian challenges.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Revenue decreased by $100 million sequentially in Q1 2025, contrasting with a 20% sequential increase in Q4 2024. Adjusted EBITDA rose from $677 million in Q4 2024 to $855 million in Q1 2025, reflecting higher aluminum prices and improved cost management.

  • Management's tone shifted from optimistic in Q4 2024, focusing on exceeding profitability program targets, to a more cautious stance in Q1 2025, addressing tariff uncertainties and operational cost challenges.

  • Analysts in Q4 2024 focused on profitability program achievements, while Q1 2025 discussions centered on the financial repercussions of tariffs and the viability of the San Ciprian smelter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified U.S. Section 232 tariffs as a significant risk, with a projected $400 million to $425 million annual cost impact. Mitigation strategies include leveraging higher Midwest premiums and adjusting trade flows.

  • The San Ciprian smelter restart poses financial risks, with expected cash consumption of $90 million to $110 million in 2025. Restricted cash release and energy contract negotiations remain unresolved.

  • Analysts questioned the potential for market disruptions due to tariffs and the financial sustainability of the San Ciprian operations.

Final Takeaway

Alcoa's Q1 2025 earnings highlighted strong operational performance and cost management improvements. However, significant concerns stem from the financial and operational impacts of U.S. tariffs on Canadian aluminum imports and the San Ciprian smelter restart. Management emphasized ongoing efforts to mitigate tariff implications and ensure strategic execution, but uncertainties remain regarding tariff persistence and market response.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: First horizon targets 15% return on tangible common equity by leveraging capital and diversified strategies
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: First Horizon Corporation (FHN) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO Bryan Jordan emphasized the company’s resilience amidst macroeconomic uncertainty and reiterated its strategic focus on safety, soundness, profitability, and growth. The Q1 2025 results reflected steady pre-provision net revenue growth, achieved through margin expansion and disciplined deposit pricing.
  • Adjusted EPS for the quarter was $0.42, down $0.01 from the previous quarter. The company repurchased $360 million of shares, reducing CET1 ratio to 10.9%. Credit performance remained strong with a charge-off ratio of 19 basis points.
  • CFO Hope Dmuchowski highlighted a $1 million increase in net interest income, driven by a 38 basis point reduction in interest-bearing deposit costs. Expenses decreased by $20 million on an adjusted basis, reflecting efficient management.

Outlook

  • Management maintained its 2025 guidance, anticipating total revenue growth within the provided range, supported by countercyclical business contributions and balanced revenue streams. The net charge-off guidance for the year remains at 15 to 25 basis points, attributed to prudent credit management.
  • The company targets a return on tangible common equity of 15% or higher in the intermediate term, driven by strategic capital deployment and a diversified business model.

Financial Results

  • The net interest margin expanded to 3.42%, supported by a 27 basis point decline in average deposit costs. This improvement offset a 20 basis point reduction in average loan yields.
  • Fee income declined $5 million, attributed to volatility in fixed income revenues and other normal fluctuations.
  • Loan balances experienced a 1% decline due to paydowns, particularly in commercial real estate, although growth in the C&I portfolio was noted.

Q&A

  • Michael Rose, Raymond James: Asked about PPNR growth and expense outlook. CFO Dmuchowski expressed confidence in 2025 guidance and mentioned that countercyclical businesses would help offset revenue pressures.
  • Jon Arfstrom, RBC Capital Markets: Queried about C&I pipeline and borrower sentiment. CEO Jordan noted cautious optimism among borrowers despite economic uncertainties.
  • Ebrahim Poonawala, Bank of America: Discussed the timeline for achieving 15% ROTCE. Management cited credit normalization, capital deployment, and economic stability as key factors.
  • Anthony Elian, JPMorgan: Asked about the impact of tariffs and trade uncertainties. Chief Credit Officer Thomas Hung acknowledged temporary borrowing pauses due to these issues but highlighted the diversified nature of the loan portfolio.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts voiced concerns about macroeconomic uncertainty, particularly around credit performance, loan growth, and deposit costs. Their tone ranged from neutral to slightly skeptical.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, particularly around their strategic initiatives and the resilience of the business model. CEO Jordan expressed optimism in achieving long-term targets.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone remained steady, emphasizing stability and adaptability in a volatile environment.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language remained consistent, with management reaffirming confidence in achieving 2025 targets.
  • Strategic priorities, including disciplined credit management and expense efficiency, continued to be emphasized.
  • Analysts remained focused on credit quality, deposit trends, and the trajectory of loan growth, with some concerns about macroeconomic pressures.
  • Management’s tone was consistent with the previous quarter, balancing optimism with caution.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited macroeconomic uncertainty and potential recession risks as key challenges, compounded by tariff impacts and market volatility.
  • Analysts raised concerns about deposit cost stabilization, loan growth in a competitive environment, and credit quality amidst economic uncertainty.
  • The company’s mitigation strategies include disciplined credit practices, diversified revenue streams, and strategic capital deployment.

Final Takeaway

First Horizon’s Q1 2025 performance showcased its ability to navigate a challenging macroeconomic environment, with a focus on margin expansion, disciplined expense management, and credit quality. Management remains confident in its strategic initiatives and targets, particularly achieving a 15% return on tangible common equity in the intermediate term.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
